segments.title: Why the Third Sea Lord could shape the whole fleet
segments.summary: The Third Sea Lord's control of construction, engineering, ordnance, dockyards, and scientific work made the office a central engine of Royal Navy policy.
segments.body: The Third Sea Lord and Controller connected fleet requirements to naval construction, engineering, ordnance, dockyards, and scientific work. That span made the office central to nearly every Admiralty discussion about whether ships could be designed, equipped, repaired, or made ready. The incumbent also had to explain spending and secret research to the Treasury, Parliament, and senior politicians. A capable officer who remained in post for years therefore built both technical knowledge and a political network, giving him leverage that could rival the First Sea Lord on procurement questions.

evidence.note: The transcript inventories the office's departments and explains its institutional and political leverage.

segments.title: Why a larger destroyer could need fewer engineers
segments.summary: Henderson answers a crewing objection by showing that a roomier machinery layout can reduce maintenance effort and engineering manpower.
segments.body: Chatfield questions whether Henderson's larger destroyers would impose an unaffordable personnel burden. Henderson's answer is that extra hull volume can make machinery easier to reach and maintain. Engineers no longer have to crawl into cramped spaces or spend as long completing the same work, so fewer people can preserve the required standard. The exchange turns maintainability into a force-structure argument: displacement can save scarce trained manpower rather than merely consume it.

evidence.note: Henderson explains that easier machinery access in a larger hull can reduce engineering workload and crew requirements.

segments.title: The modern procurement lesson of restoring a naval integrator
segments.summary: The historical office is linked to a modern need for one naval officer to connect future requirements, acquisition, and political explanation.
segments.body: The lecture argues that Royal Navy procurement became progressively more troubled after the traditional Third Sea Lord and Controller structure disappeared. Recent efforts to recreate an equivalent coordinating role are presented as an improvement because responsibility is clearer. One officer can look ahead, decide what the service must procure, and connect those needs to the wider program. The same officer can then explain both the requirement and its timing to political decision-makers, reducing the gaps between naval planning, acquisition, and advocacy.

evidence.note: The transcript connects restoration of a coordinating naval role with improved future planning and political explanation.

segments.title: Fisher's speed doctrine and the limits of Renown
segments.summary: Fisher's early support for destroyers and faster battle lines is tested against the short-lived speed advantage purchased for HMS Renown.
segments.body: Fisher's claim that speed could substitute for armor had real force when a few knots represented a large proportional advantage. As Third Sea Lord he also balanced new ideas with conventional fleet needs more readily than he later did as First Sea Lord. HMS Renown nevertheless shows the risk of buying speed with protection and firepower: her 19 knots briefly outpaced the Majestic class, but the Canopus and Formidable classes rapidly narrowed the gap while carrying heavier guns. Improving machinery could therefore erase a speed advantage within a few years while the original sacrifices in armor and armament remained built into the ship.

evidence.note: Fisher's destroyer work, speed thesis, and the Renown comparison are discussed in detail.

segments.title: Why the Third Sea Lord could plan beyond a personal legacy
segments.summary: The office's place in an officer's career encouraged compromise and long-range development rather than a departing First Sea Lord's rush for legacy.
segments.body: A First Sea Lord knows that retirement is close and may feel pressure to secure a visible legacy before leaving office. The Third Sea Lord usually has more of a career ahead, often moving to a fleet command before returning to a still higher Admiralty post. That longer horizon makes compromise easier because one program does not have to settle every question immediately. The contrast helps explain why Fisher could be more balanced as Controller than in his later service leadership, and why the office rewarded patient institutional development.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts the First Sea Lord's limited time with the Third Sea Lord's longer career horizon and greater willingness to compromise.

segments.title: Fisher's forward-fire doctrine before long-range gunnery
segments.summary: Fisher's preference for six guns firing ahead reflects an era when rapid 6-inch fire did most of the expected damage and heavy guns supplied the finish.
segments.body: Fisher studies Tsushima and favors enough forward-firing weapons to establish the range while a fleet closes. In that tactical setting, rapidly firing 6-inch guns are expected to produce most of the damage. The heavy battery has prestige and coup-de-grace value, but it is not yet assumed to deliver a coordinated long-range engagement by itself. The logic changes as fire control improves, making this passage a useful marker between close-range mixed-caliber doctrine and the later big-gun battle line.

evidence.note: Fisher's forward-fire preference is tied to Tsushima, rapid 6-inch fire, and the later rise of coordinated long-range gunnery.

segments.title: How HMS Unicorn passed as an aviation support ship
segments.summary: Henderson uses classification, design details, and even the naming process to move a full-flight-deck carrier through institutional constraints.
segments.body: HMS Unicorn is officially presented as a forward aviation support ship rather than an aircraft carrier. Henderson gives the proposal enough supporting and repair functions to respect the rules while retaining a full flight deck and an operationally useful carrier form. Structures initially associated with the support role disappear as the design develops, revealing how carefully the requirement was framed. The ship's name also illustrates the Controller's reach because the naming committee effectively answered to the Third Sea Lord during this period.

evidence.note: The transcript explains Unicorn's forward-aviation-support designation, full flight deck, changing structures, and naming authority.

segments.title: Henderson's carrier doctrine and rearmament portfolio
segments.summary: Henderson combined carrier expertise with a broad program spanning cruisers, destroyers, escorts, submarines, shipyards, and wartime design studies.
segments.body: As Rear Admiral Aircraft Carriers, Henderson helped rewrite British doctrine for multi-carrier operations and fleet air defense. He then carried that operational knowledge into the Controller's office, where he pursued the return of naval aviation to Royal Navy control. The conflict is presented less as a simple Navy-versus-RAF quarrel than as a struggle with an Air Ministry captured by one faction, a distinction that explains why some air officers also resisted its policy. Henderson's portfolio extended beyond carriers to Town and Dido cruisers, Tribal and Hunt destroyers, Flower-class corvettes, U-class submarines, and underwater hydrodynamics. The breadth of that output shows a Controller preparing a balanced wartime fleet rather than championing one favored ship type.

evidence.note: The transcript connects Henderson's carrier command, multi-carrier doctrine, aviation politics, and broad cruiser, destroyer, escort, and submarine portfolio.

segments.title: Saving shipyards while rearmament consumed its planners
segments.summary: Henderson fights Treasury-backed yard closures and works himself into fatal illness, exposing both the industrial and human costs of rebuilding naval readiness.
segments.body: Henderson takes office just after the formal end of the Ten Year Rule, while tight Treasury control still constrains real rearmament. He has to preserve civilian shipyards against a policy that pays them to close and binds them not to reopen, making industrial capacity part of immediate war planning. Long days, late nights, and weekend yard inspections allow him to drive the program forward but damage his health. His death before the war also contributes to a shortage of experienced senior officers, helping explain why commanders such as Somerville and Cunningham later carry such punishing workloads.

evidence.note: The transcript covers Treasury-backed shipyard closures, Henderson's exhausting work routine, his death, and the resulting senior-officer shortage.

segments.title: Why Henderson stockpiled designs before war
segments.summary: Repeated Lion, Vanguard, and carrier studies created a reserve of mature options that could be adapted quickly when wartime left little time for design from scratch.
segments.body: The Lion class was not simply an unready design; wartime pauses encouraged repeated changes until restarting with a new design became more sensible than completing the original ships. Henderson also kept Vanguard concepts and other contingency studies available for changing circumstances. The method assumed that wartime would not allow leisurely design work, so promising options had to be explored and rerun in peace. Carrier studies repeatedly returned to a 27,000-ton ideal combining armor, subdivision, and a strike-sized air group, then documented the compromises needed for particular programs. Many apparently sudden wartime ships could therefore draw on several years of prior investigation instead of appearing from nowhere.

evidence.note: The transcript explains Lion redesigns, the Vanguard reserve, repeated contingency studies, and the recurring 27,000-ton carrier ideal.

segments.title: How requirements became Royal Navy warships
segments.summary: A concise walkthrough shows the Third Sea Lord turning fleet requirements and scientific forecasts into options, detailed designs, yard assignments, and funded construction.
segments.body: The Board of Admiralty could ask for a new class and receive several options from the Third Sea Lord, including two closely matched serious choices and one clearly unsuitable comparator. Once the Board selected an option, the Controller supervised detailed design, chose yards, managed construction, and approved technical revisions without returning every change to the full Board. The process could also begin inside the Controller's departments when scientific reviews and fleet exercises revealed a future requirement. In that path, the same office framed the need, prepared the design choices, ran the acquisition, and secured political funding. The sequence shows why the post joined technical authority with unusual agenda-setting power.

evidence.note: The transcript traces both Board-initiated and Third Sea Lord-initiated procurement paths.

segments.title: Henderson arrives on the technological cusp of 1892
segments.summary: Placed in 1892 with later knowledge, Henderson could fund turbines, boilers, guns, and reform allies before the naval race hardened.
segments.body: The scenario gives Henderson his later knowledge but places him only a few decades before the technology on which he trained, making acceleration more plausible than miraculous invention. He understands gunnery, torpedoes, engineering, carrier operations, and the Victorian service culture well enough to identify useful people and feasible next steps. His first institutional problem is finding or preserving an operationally minded First Sea Lord, with George Tryon offered as a possible ally if the fatal 1893 maneuver can be averted. He must then direct money toward Parsons, Brown, Yarrow, boiler research, guns, and other foundations of the coming naval revolution. Six years are enough to lay foundations, but his memory of World War I gives those years an urgent purpose.

evidence.note: The scenario sets out Henderson's 1892 political allies, technical opportunities, and time advantage.

segments.title: Carrier operations help define Dido and Tribal requirements
segments.summary: Henderson's carrier staff turns the practical problem of chasing wind into escort requirements and an early task-group doctrine.
segments.body: Before becoming Third Sea Lord, Henderson spends three years working with the existing Controller on the material needs of naval aviation. Early thinking assumes that a carrier may have to leave the formation to chase the wind required for flying operations. Dido cruisers and Tribal destroyers begin partly as answers to the need for escorts able to accompany and protect that movement. Doctrine then evolves toward having the task group maneuver with the carrier, showing how operating experience can generate both new formations and new ship requirements.

evidence.note: The transcript links carrier wind-chasing and task-group evolution to early Dido and Tribal escort concepts.

segments.title: Turrets, turbines, and fire control reshape the Victorian fleet
segments.summary: Henderson's likely priorities replace casemates with turrets, accelerate turbine machinery, introduce director fire control, and tighten quality control.
segments.body: World War I experience would make Henderson suspicious of wet casemate batteries and push him toward turreted secondary armament for cruisers and capital ships. He would not need to be persuaded that turbines offered advantages over triple-expansion machinery, so destroyers and a redesigned Canopus class could become early test beds. His memory of Jutland also favors centralized director fire control and systematic attention to armor, gun, shell, and fuze quality. The resulting ships might resemble 1920s design practice decades early, with more useful firing arcs and machinery installed around a coherent tactical system. Henderson could sell those advances as efficiency measures as well as combat improvements, making technical modernization easier to defend politically.

evidence.note: The transcript proposes turreted cruisers and capital ships, turbine propulsion, director control, and stricter quality assurance.

segments.title: Turreted secondary guns become a manpower reform
segments.summary: Replacing sixteen casemate guns with eight turreted weapons preserves a broadside while sharply reducing gun crews.
segments.body: Henderson can defend turreted secondary armament in financial and personnel terms rather than relying only on combat theory. Sixteen single casemate guns require crews for weapons that cannot all bear on one side at once. Eight guns in twin turrets can deliver the same eight-gun broadside while roughly halving the number of gun positions to man. The example shows how a design change can improve weather protection and firing arcs while answering the Admiralty's chronic concern about trained manpower.

evidence.note: The transcript compares sixteen casemate guns with eight turreted guns that retain the broadside but require fewer crews.

segments.title: Convertible tanks exploit treaty displacement rules
segments.summary: Feed-water tanks that could become fuel tanks illustrate Henderson's habit of extracting wartime capability from the letter of naval rules.
segments.body: Henderson's institutional maneuvering appears in small technical details as well as major ship programs. Some carrier and cruiser feed-water tanks could be converted to carry fuel in wartime. Feed water did not count toward treaty standard displacement in the way fuel did, so the arrangement preserved legal peacetime measurements while reserving additional endurance. The example is a compact lesson in how naval architects and administrators could use dual-purpose spaces to reconcile formal limits with operational needs.

evidence.note: The transcript notes that feed-water tanks could become wartime fuel tanks because the two liquids were treated differently in standard displacement.

segments.title: A second Warrior moment through science and deterrence
segments.summary: The scenario's largest payoff may be an early leap in fire control, aviation, propulsion, metallurgy, and radio detection that deters rivals rather than merely winning a race.
segments.body: Henderson enters an era without naval treaties and with a Treasury less able to constrain his departments, giving him unusual room to fund experimentation. He could recognize promising aircraft, engine, metallurgy, fire-control, and radio-detection work and place it under organizations he already knew how to direct. Early researchers such as Marconi would receive attention decades before comparable naval funding historically arrived, though their equipment would still have to develop from contemporary science. The argument is therefore not that 1930s systems appear instantly, but that informed selection and sustained investment accelerate several connected fields. A sufficiently visible lead could create another HMS Warrior moment in which deterrent effect buys time as well as battlefield advantage.

evidence.note: The transcript links Henderson's institutional knowledge to aviation, radar precursors, and a deterrent technological leap.

segments.title: Henderson's credit-free method of naval reform
segments.summary: Henderson's technical knowledge and willingness to let others take credit could make him more effective than Fisher inside Victorian naval politics.
segments.body: A speculative 15,000-ton, turbine-powered ship with six 13.5-inch guns illustrates the kind of dramatic but technically directed leap Henderson might pursue. His knowledge of later twin and triple turrets, large guns, armor, and propulsion would help him make ambitious demands without losing sight of what Victorian industry could build. The more durable legacy would be systematic quality control for armor, weapons, shells, fuzes, and machinery, because those standards would shape every later class. Politically, Henderson is portrayed as willing to let allies claim public credit if that secured their backing. World War I had taught him that lives, readiness, and national safety mattered more than reputation, making quiet coalition-building a powerful reform method.

evidence.note: The transcript evaluates Henderson's proposed ship, quality priorities, and understated political style.

segments.title: A qualitative race changes Anglo-German strategy
segments.summary: An early British technical leap might force Tirpitz to compete in ship quality instead of numbers, altering budgets, alliances, and the road to the Entente.
segments.body: A dramatic early British lead could force other navies to compete in individual ship quality rather than hull numbers. Germany would struggle to fund both a technical contest and Tirpitz's historical fleet-law expansion without taking resources from an army that would win the budget argument. If the quantitative Anglo-German challenge never develops in the same form, British leaders may feel less pressure to align against Germany. France, the United States, Italy, and Japan would also have to decide whether and how to enter the quality race. The naval counterfactual therefore reaches beyond ship characteristics to budgets, alliance choices, and the diplomatic road to the Entente.

evidence.note: The transcript explores how a quality contest could disrupt Tirpitz's numerical strategy and British alignment.

segments.title: Fisher confronts treaties, the Treasury, and the Air Ministry
segments.summary: Moved into the 1930s, Fisher faces mature technology but a far denser political system of treaty limits, fiscal control, and divided aviation authority.
segments.body: Fisher arrives in a 1930s system constrained by naval treaties, fiscal policy, and a separate Air Ministry. Technologies that were speculative during his original career are now mature enough to impose their own institutions, budgets, and professional expertise. He cannot simply repeat an Edwardian program because the political obstacles are denser and the available weapons work differently. The swap therefore reverses Henderson's challenge: instead of bringing later technical knowledge into a looser political world, Fisher brings forceful politics into a period whose technology he must first learn.

evidence.note: The transcript introduces the treaty, Treasury, Air Ministry, and mature-technology constraints facing Fisher in the 1930s.

segments.title: Aircraft turn Fisher's speed doctrine into long-range firepower
segments.summary: Treating carrier aircraft as artillery lets Fisher apply 'speed is life' to strike range and aircraft performance rather than to marginal gains in ship speed.
segments.body: From a fleet officer's perspective, carrier aircraft can be understood as artillery whose projectiles are delivered at the aircraft's operating radius. The comparison shifts the decisive range from a gun's reach to the reach of the air group. Fisher's belief that speed preserves initiative has diminishing returns for already-fast ships, but it remains powerful for aircraft before guided missiles. Faster aircraft can strike farther, react sooner, and survive more readily, giving his old doctrine a new and potentially more productive technological outlet.

evidence.note: The transcript describes aircraft as long-range naval artillery and applies Fisher's speed doctrine to aviation.

segments.title: Fisher attacks the Air Ministry and Treasury head-on
segments.summary: The same confrontational style that made Fisher effective in Edwardian politics could make the 1930s aviation and spending battles exceptionally disruptive.
segments.body: Henderson advances naval aviation through private agreements and careful institutional compromise. Fisher is expected to confront the Air Ministry, Treasury, and political direction of naval policy openly rather than thread the same needle. His willingness to destroy internal opponents and take arguments into public politics could break an impasse that quieter methods only manage. It could also make the reform process bloody and unstable, because he enters a much more crowded political landscape than the one in which his original methods developed.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts Henderson's subtle approach with Fisher's head-on political style and the harder institutional landscape of the 1930s.

segments.title: Fisher still needs engineers to turn concepts into ships
segments.summary: Fisher's conceptual energy depends on constructors such as Stanley Goodall, and his Dreadnought turret choice shows that he can misunderstand unfamiliar engineering.
segments.body: Fisher generates powerful concepts but relies on naval constructors and engineers such as Stanley Goodall to make them workable. The lecture uses HMS Dreadnought's wing turrets as a warning: Fisher accepted them because he believed superfiring arrangements were not technically feasible. Once corrected, he recognized that a narrower superfiring layout could save a turret, reduce beam, and perhaps increase speed. In the 1930s he would again need time to learn the machinery, aviation, and weapon technologies that had matured beyond his experience before his political energy could produce sound designs.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses Fisher's dependence on constructors, his error about superfiring turrets, and the need to assess unfamiliar 1930s technology.

segments.title: Fisher turns treaty limits into a public political battle
segments.summary: Fisher's press tactics and appetite for unconstrained designs make the Second London Naval Treaty unlikely to survive in its historical form.
segments.body: Fisher is unlikely to accept the Second London Naval Treaty's 14-inch gun limit or its restrictions on individual ships. Where Henderson quietly briefed ministers and legislators, Fisher could connect treaty constraints to unemployment, closed yards, and national danger in repeated conversations with trade union figures, press proprietors, and politicians. His public style would make naval limitation a domestic political battle rather than a technical negotiation inside government. The likely result is either no Second London agreement or an early renegotiation that leaves designers far more freedom. Even a formally surviving treaty would have to accommodate a Controller who regarded a constrained 27,000-ton design as an invitation to demand something larger.

evidence.note: The transcript predicts Fisher's public lobbying against the Second London Naval Treaty and its individual ship limits.

segments.title: Fleet Air Arm control becomes a fight over budgets and doctrine
segments.summary: The Air Ministry's hold on naval aviation protects funding and manufacturers for its dominant bombing faction, giving Fisher an institutional target rather than a simple interservice rivalry.
segments.body: The Air Ministry has practical reasons to retain the Fleet Air Arm, including avoiding competition for money and aircraft manufacturers. Some officers also sincerely believe unified air control produces better air power. The problem described here is factional capture: Bomber Command's advocates dominate the ministry and discount other operational requirements, including naval aviation. Fisher's instinct to attack opponents at their weakest point could turn the return of Fleet Air Arm control into an unusually aggressive contest over budgets, industrial capacity, and doctrine.

evidence.note: The transcript explains Air Ministry funding and manufacturer incentives, Bomber Command's dominance, and Fisher's likely response.

segments.title: A weak escalator clause empties the treaty of restraint
segments.summary: If Fisher accepts a treaty at all, its ship and gun limits likely come with an escalator clause easy enough to trigger that designers can escape them quickly.
segments.body: The Second London framework had already abandoned cumulative fleet limits in favor of restrictions on individual ships and guns. Fisher is unlikely to tolerate even those boundaries unless an escalator clause makes them easy to discard. In the counterfactual, evidence that another navy is considering a larger design could be enough to justify escalation. That would preserve the diplomatic appearance of limitation while allowing the Royal Navy to move rapidly from King George V compromises toward Lion-sized battleships and larger carriers.

evidence.note: The transcript suggests Fisher would accept individual limits only with an escalator clause that could be triggered very easily.

segments.title: Fisher chooses larger battleships and carriers
segments.summary: Freed from strict treaty limits, Fisher likely substitutes Lion-like battleships and Implacable-like carriers for the historical compromises of the late 1930s.
segments.body: Without strict individual limits, the King George V class would probably move toward the larger Lion concept. The same freedom changes carrier design because the 27,000-ton Ark Royal and Illustrious studies already combined armor, underwater protection, subdivision, and a useful strike air group. Those studies resemble the later Implacable class closely enough to offer Fisher a credible starting point rather than a blank sheet. His preference for conspicuous capability would therefore replace several historical treaty compromises with larger ships whose characteristics had already been explored. The result is not unlimited fantasy construction, but an earlier choice of designs the Royal Navy had judged desirable and set aside for political reasons.

evidence.note: The transcript compares treaty-free battleships with the Lions and 27,000-ton carrier studies with the later Implacables.

segments.title: Fisher recruits Dominion yards for escort construction
segments.summary: An 'imperial police' program could distribute sloop and corvette orders across Dominion yards while preserving British capacity for larger warships.
segments.body: Fisher cannot accelerate every British program by scrapping old ships because the treaty years have already reduced that reserve. He can instead ask Dominion governments and administrations to build sloops, corvettes, and other escorts in their own yards. Branding the effort as an imperial police or common-defense program would make local participation politically attractive while releasing British yards for more demanding work. The idea draws on Fisher's historical success at using Dominion politics and press coverage to secure contributions to shared naval programs.

evidence.note: The transcript proposes using Dominion yards for sloops and corvettes under a politically attractive imperial program.

segments.title: Fisher makes himself more dangerous outside government
segments.summary: By feeding journalists a heroic naval narrative, Fisher can make dismissal politically riskier than keeping his disruptive energy inside the Admiralty.
segments.body: Fisher understands that the press dislikes an unanswered vacuum and supplies a compelling story in which naval investment protects workers, the nation, and the empire. Journalists can then present themselves as crusaders against accountants and officials who appear to obstruct safety and employment. This strategy gives Fisher a distinctive form of job security: ministers fear that an unshackled outsider would create even more trouble than the Controller they can partially constrain. Occasional concessions reinforce that belief by suggesting that only office disciplines him. Henderson becomes indispensable through competence, while Fisher survives by making his political removal look more dangerous than his continued appointment.

evidence.note: The transcript explains Fisher's press narratives, his double-term precedent, and political job security based on being more dangerous outside office.

segments.title: Would Fisher choose aviation or battleship primacy?
segments.summary: Fisher would fund battleships, carriers, destroyers, and submarines, but his speed doctrine may ultimately make aviation the fleet's primary striking arm.
segments.body: Fisher would still build battleships, carriers, destroyers, and submarines because all four fit concerns he had expressed during his original career. The decisive question is which system becomes the fleet's primary striking arm and which serves in support. Battleship primacy leads toward escalating gun calibers and a renewed battlecruiser logic. Carrier primacy retains modernized 15-inch-gun ships but directs the most aggressive development toward aircraft, escorts, and underwater attack. Because speed offers more operational return in aircraft than in already-fast ships, the lecture judges aviation the likelier winner.

evidence.note: The transcript weighs battleship and carrier primacy while retaining roles for destroyers and submarines, then judges aviation the likelier focus.

segments.title: Town-class cruisers connect rearmament with employment
segments.summary: Henderson quietly placed cruiser work in distressed cities; Fisher could turn the same jobs argument into a national campaign against treaty restraint.
segments.body: Naval limitation remained associated with peace, but the Depression also created pressure to replace unemployment support with shipbuilding work. Henderson used that opening quietly when arguing for the number, size, and yard distribution of Town-class cruisers. Orders could be placed in cities most affected by economic conditions, making rearmament an employment measure as well as a fleet program. Fisher would likely amplify the same logic through the press, publicly trapping ministers between treaty observance, industrial recovery, and claims of national danger.

evidence.note: The transcript links Town-class orders to distressed cities and employment, then contrasts Henderson's quiet method with Fisher's public campaign style.

segments.title: Gun upgrades or faster torpedo bombers define Fisher's fleet
segments.summary: The final branch contrasts 15-inch modernization and ever-larger battleship guns with an early Fleet Air Arm transfer focused on engines, speed, and long-range torpedo attack.
segments.body: If carriers become primary, Fisher might build new ships with nine improved 15-inch guns while modernizing older 15-inch battleships and even considering a Nelson-class rearmament. If battleships dominate, the first generation may begin at 16 inches and grow as soon as treaty restraint collapses. An earlier return of Fleet Air Arm control to the Navy creates the opposite investment pattern. Fisher would pour money into aircraft engines because faster, longer-ranged torpedo bombers fit both his speed doctrine and the period's preferred anti-ship weapon. The two paths give other navies very different problems: an accelerating gun race or a British lead in carrier strike range and reaction speed.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts modernized 15-inch ships and larger guns with earlier naval aviation control, engine investment, and fast torpedo bombers.
